title: Chapter 32: Iris's War Cry
content: The ritual was halfway cast when the Hollow Council came for me.

I heard them before I saw them — a sound like breath drawn through broken teeth, a collective inhalation that pulled the warmth from the air and left the night sharpened to a killing edge. The fog that had been clinging to the forest floor rose. Not gradually, not in the slow drift I'd grown accustomed to, but all at once, like a curtain yanked upward on a stage. And behind it: seven figures.

Grey. Featureless. Moving in formation with the synchronized precision of wolves who have hunted together so long they no longer need signals. They emerged from the tree line and crossed the clearing toward the Boundary Stones in a line that bent at the center, a crescent of smoke and malice, and the temperature dropped so sharply that my fingers went numb on the chalk I'd been using to complete the circle.

The ritual circle. Half-finished. Seven stones humming with the echoes of six ancestress spirits and one living woman trying to weave them all into a single working that would break two and a half centuries of compounded theft.
And seven grey figures walking straight through it.
"MAREN!" I screamed.

Because I felt it. The moment the first Hollow Council member crossed the threshold of the circle, something *shifted* — a law I hadn't known existed clicking into place like a padlock. The suppression law. Written into the curse itself, embedded so deeply I'd never found it in the journals because none of the ancestresses had lived long enough to document it. *Ancestral ghosts cannot manifest in the same space as the Hollow Council.*
Not "should not." Not "are discouraged from." *Cannot.*

Maren's presence dimmed. I felt her — the cool authority of her, the steady weight of a woman who had held a household together with nothing but willpower and a voice like a closed door — and then she was gone. Not departed. Not willing. *Pushed back*. Like a hand pressed flat against glass, visible but unable to pass through. I could still sense her shape behind the barrier the law had erected, but I couldn't reach her. The thread between us went cold.

Cordelia's golden warmth thinned to a whisper. Hazel's flickering, anxious light stuttered twice and went out like a candle in a draft. One by one, the ancestress spirits — the voices I'd spent weeks learning, the women whose gifts I carried in my blood — were forced backward, compressed into some space I couldn't access, pressed into a corner of existence the Council had labeled "not allowed."
The ritual was failing.

I could feel it unraveling. The framework I'd built, the seven-component machine that Nadine had drafted and I was trying to complete — it was coming apart at the seams. Without the spirits, the gifts had no anchor. Voice-Binding needed Marguerite's authority. Rage Fire needed Célestine's fury. Each component was a circuit that required its original source, and the Council was systematically cutting every wire.
I couldn't stop casting.

That was the trap. That was the beautiful, vicious geometry of their timing — they'd waited until I was past the point of no return. If I stopped now, I'd lose the circle. The chalk lines would break. The resonance between the seven stones would collapse. And once the circle broke, I couldn't redraw it. The alignment was specific — celestial, elemental, tied to a configuration that wouldn't recur for another nine months. If I lost the circle, I lost the stones. If I lost the stones, I lost everything. Every journal. Every gift. Every dead woman's testimony. Nine months of planning and two hundred and sixty years of suffering — gone.
I had to keep casting while under attack.

My hands moved on their own, muscle memory and desperation, completing the next line of the working while my eyes tracked the seven grey figures spreading around me. The closest was ten feet away. I could see the hollows of what might have been eyes, might have been empty sockets, might have been nothing at all — just the impression of a face where no face existed. The fog curled around its edges like a living garment, and the smell of it hit me: ozone, sharp and electric, underlaid with something that was almost sweet. Almost floral. The residue of other people's magic, leeched and repurposed. The Council didn't generate power. They harvested it. And they'd harvested enough to fill a killing field.
My chalk hand trembled. The line I was drawing wavered. I steadied it with my other hand and kept going, half my attention on the working, half on the circle of grey death closing around me.

The second Council member moved. Then the third. They were positioning — not attacking yet, just arranging themselves, each one occupying a point on the circle like dancers taking their marks. They were going to snuff the working out from seven directions simultaneously. Clean. Efficient. Nothing left but ash and silence.

Maren slammed against the barrier from the other side. I felt the impact like a hand against my chest — not painful, but *desperate*. She was trying. They were all trying. Six women pushing against a law that said they weren't allowed to exist here, six fists pounding on a door held shut by the very authority that had silenced them in life.
And none of them could break through.

I drew the next line. The chalk scraped against stone, and the sound of it was so small, so absurdly ordinary against the vast grey wrongness pressing in, that I almost laughed. Almost. My throat was tight. My jaw ached from clenching. The taste in my mouth was ash — bitter and dry, coating my tongue, the residue of a ritual that was burning itself out.
And then.
And then something changed.

Not a sound. Not a movement. A *temperature*. A shift in the quality of the air itself, the way the atmosphere alters just before a wildfire finds its second wind. The ash taste in my mouth went from bitter to *charged*, electric, like licking a battery. The hair on my arms stood up. The down on my forearms prickled, and my skin knew before my mind did what was coming.
One of the spirits was not going quietly.
Iris.

I'd felt her before — of course I had. She was the angriest of them all. The most volatile. The one whose emotions came through the ancestral thread not as impressions but as *assaults*, sharp and hot and sometimes painful, a woman who burned so constantly that simply being near her memory felt like standing too close to a forge. In life, they'd called her hysterical. They'd diagnosed her with melancholia and neurasthenia and nervous exhaustion and every other clinical term men had invented to repackage a woman's suffering as a personal defect. They'd told her to calm down. They'd told her to moderate her tone. They'd told her that her grief was excessive, her anger was unbecoming, her passion was a symptom. They'd institutionalized her twice. They'd buried her in an unmarked grave.
And now the suppression law — the Council's law, the law that said she could not exist in this space — was trying to push her back.
She refused.

I felt it. Not the gentle resistance of a woman testing a boundary but the full-throated *assault* of a woman who had been told to be quiet for two hundred and sixty years and was done. Done with silence. Done with dimming. Done with the slow, patient, institutionalized erasure that had reduced her from a person to a diagnosis to a footnote to nothing.

The law pressed against her. She pressed back. The law said: *You cannot manifest.* And Iris Buena, who had been hospitalized for the crime of being angry, who had been diagnosed with diseases invented to silence her, who had been called hysterical and unbalanced and unfit and too much — Iris Buena heard that law and she *burned*.

Not because she was more powerful than the other spirits. She wasn't. Her gift was modest — a minor talent for flame, the kind of thing that could light a hearth or singe a hem. But her gift WAS fire. And fire doesn't obey suppression laws. Fire doesn't file petitions. Fire doesn't wait for permission. Fire doesn't calm down.
Fire *spreads*.
Iris manifested.

She erupted from the space between the stones like a blue flare, and the sound she made — the SOUND — I will carry it in my bones until I die. It wasn't a scream. A scream is what you do when you're afraid. This was a war cry. It was the sound of a train derailing. It was the sound of the ocean throwing itself against a cliff face. It was the sound of a woman who had swallowed two centuries of silence and was finally, finally, *finally* opening her throat.

The blue flame took shape. A woman — solid, tangible, present in a way no ghost had been in that clearing since the Council arrived. She stood between me and the nearest grey figure and she was *real*. Nineteenth-century dress, dark and severe, the kind of mourning clothes that were supposed to make grief invisible. Her hair was wild — not carefully arranged, not pinned and pomaded, but FREE, a corona of dark curls lit from within by blue fire. Her eyes were not eyes. They were furnaces. They were the heart of the flame, white-blue and terrible.

And her voice — oh god, her voice — it rolled across the clearing like thunder, like a wave, like something that had been dammed for two hundred and sixty years and had just discovered that dams are only strong as long as the water stays patient.
"YOU CALLED MY PAIN HYSTERIA."

She roared it. Not said. Not declaimed. *Roared*. The sound hit me in the chest like a physical force and I staggered, and the chalk dropped from my fingers, and I didn't care. The ritual could wait. The world could wait. This woman was speaking.
"YOU TOLD ME MY GRIEF WAS DISEASE."

The nearest Council member recoiled. The grey figure bent backward like a sapling in a hurricane wind, and the fog that composed it shredded and reformed, shredded and reformed, struggling to hold its shape against the force of her.
"YOU SAID MY ANGER WAS UNLADYLIKE."

She stepped forward. Each footfall left a blue burn on the ground, and the grass ignited beneath her — not yellow-orange fire but blue, the impossible blue of a flame that burns hotter than nature allows, the blue of a rage so concentrated it has transcended color.
"NOW FEEL WHAT HYSTERIA LOOKS LIKE."
She spread her arms.
The forest ignited.

Not the whole forest — a ring. A perfect ring of blue flame erupting from the ground in a circle around the Boundary Stones, twenty feet across, fire climbing the trunks of the closest pines in lines of blue that raced upward like sap running in reverse. The heat hit me like a wall. Not the dry, suffocating heat of the Council's oppression but something *alive*, a heat with a pulse, a heat that carried fury in every degree. I could feel it on my face, on my hands, in the roots of my hair — the warmth of a woman's rage finally, finally allowed to burn.

The smell was indescribable. Ozone and sage. Burning fog — and I didn't know fog could burn until that moment, didn't know that the Council's weapon had a flammability threshold, but apparently everything burns if you get it hot enough. The fog ignited around the council members in wisps of blue-tinged vapor, and the smell layered over itself: the electric sharpness of ozone, the green cleanliness of sage, and underneath it all, the iron-and-mineral scent of earth laid bare. The combined smell of something that had been suppressed for a very long time finally being released.
Iris stood in the center of her ring of fire and she was *magnificent*.

The grey figures retreated. All seven of them, pulling back from the flames, their neat formation dissolving into something ragged and uncertain. The suppression law flickered — I felt it stutter, felt the pressure behind it waver as Iris's fire chewed at its edges. The law was real. The law was binding. But the law had not accounted for this — for a woman so angry that anger itself became the medium of her existence, for a ghost whose grief was so heavy it had become fuel, for someone who had been told to be quiet so many times that she had become LOUD.

Maren pushed against the barrier. I felt her strain. But the law held. Iris had broken through on a technicality — her gift was fire, and the law suppressed ghosts, not flames, and Iris Buena was both and neither. The other spirits didn't have that loophole. They were ghosts of voice and gold and shadow and anchor and impatience, and the law that said *you may not exist here* pressed against them as firmly as ever.
Iris held the line.

She stood between me and the Council with her arms spread wide and her blue-fire dress trailing on the ground and her wild hair crackling with light, and she was one woman holding off seven. One lion against seven wolves. The wolves couldn't cross the ring of fire — every time a grey figure tried, Iris's flame leaped toward it, hungry and precise, and the figure recoiled. But she couldn't advance either. She could hold her ground, hold the circle, hold me safe — but she couldn't push them back, couldn't drive them off, couldn't end this.

And she was burning. I could see it. The blue flame that composed her was bright and terrible, but it was also *spending*. Each burst of fire cost her something. Each pulse of defensive heat was a withdrawal from a finite account. She was using herself up — her ghost, her essence, her two-hundred-and-sixty-year accumulated fury — and she was doing it willingly, joyfully, because this was what she'd been saving herself for. This was what hysterical looked like.

The other spirits were trying. I could feel them — Maren's cool determination, Cordelia's golden frustration, Hazel's flickering desperation, and two more: faint, far-off, pushing against a barrier that wouldn't yield. Five women struggling to be *present*. Five fists against a door held shut by a law that predated their deaths. The law was simple. Elegant. Devastating. *Ancestral ghosts cannot manifest in the same space as the Hollow Council.* As long as the Council occupied this clearing, the other spirits were locked out. As long as Iris was the only one who could loophole her way through, she was alone on the front line. And she was burning.
I picked up the chalk.

My hands were steady now. Steadier than they had any right to be. The ring of blue fire crackled around me, Iris's war cry still echoing in the air, and I resumed the ritual because that was what she was buying me — time, space, the chance to finish what I'd started. I drew the next line. Then the next. The framework was there, half-complete, waiting for me to close the circuits.
But my mind was working faster than my hands.

Iris held the line. She held it with fire and fury and two centuries of being told to calm down. But five women were still trapped behind a law that said they couldn't exist in the same space as their oppressors. And the law — I could feel its architecture now, could trace its bones the way I'd traced the bones of the curse, could see the shape of the mechanism that kept my ancestresses locked in a cage made of someone else's authority.
The law said ancestral ghosts couldn't manifest in the Council's space.
I drew the next line, and I understood.

The law was jurisdictional. It defined *whose space* this was. Right now, this clearing belonged to the Hollow Council — their presence had activated the suppression, their authority had claimed the territory. The spirits couldn't manifest because they were in enemy territory.
But what if the territory changed hands?
What if this wasn't the Council's space anymore?
What if I made it *mine*?

I looked at Iris — blazing, furious, *magnificent* Iris, holding the line with nothing but rage and a gift that refused to be silenced — and I looked at the ritual circle half-drawn at my feet, and I looked at the seven grey figures circling beyond the ring of blue flame, and I made a decision that would change everything.
The law says I can't manifest ghosts in the Council's space.
But what if I made the Council exist in *mine*?
I drew the next line. And the chalk didn't just mark the stone. It *claimed* it.
